title: What To Do After a Motorcycle Accident
metaTitle: What To Do After a Motorcycle Accident — Step-by-Step
metaDescription: Motorcycle accident victims face unique challenges including bias and severe injuries. Learn the critical steps to protect your health and your claim.
description: Motorcycle accidents produce some of the most severe injuries of any road accident — and riders often face an unfair bias from insurers and juries who assume they were riding recklessly. Knowing what to do immediately, how to document your injuries, and how to counter that bias is critical to protecting your options after a crash.

sections.heading: Assess Injuries and Call for Help Immediately
sections.content: Motorcycle riders are far more vulnerable than occupants of enclosed vehicles — even a low-speed crash can result in road rash, fractures, traumatic brain injuries, or internal damage. Do not remove your helmet at the scene unless trained medical personnel instruct you to, as helmet removal can worsen spinal injuries. Call 911 and accept emergency medical evaluation even if you feel you can walk it off. Adrenaline is a powerful pain masker, and delayed-onset injuries are extremely common in motorcycle crashes. Many riders who decline an ambulance discover significant injuries only after the adrenaline subsides — sometimes hours later.
tips: Do not remove your own helmet — let paramedics handle this if a spinal injury is possible
Accept emergency medical evaluation even if you feel okay at the scene
Road rash and impact injuries can involve deeper tissue damage not visible externally

sections.heading: Document the Scene Thoroughly
sections.content: Photograph everything before your motorcycle is moved: the final position of all vehicles, skid marks, road conditions, debris, traffic signals, and signage. Photograph your protective gear — helmet, jacket, gloves — as damage to gear demonstrates the force of impact and your safety-conscious riding behavior. Get the license plates, insurance information, and driver's license details of all involved drivers. Gather witness contact information, as independent witnesses can counter any later claims of rider fault. Note whether the other driver admits to not seeing you, was on a phone, or ran a red light. Write down your speed, lane position, and what you observed before the collision while the details are fresh.
tips: Photograph your damaged helmet and gear — this is direct evidence of impact force
Note any admission by the other driver such as 'I didn't see you'
Witness testimony is especially valuable in motorcycle cases where rider-fault bias is common

sections.heading: Understanding Rider Bias — and How to Counter It
sections.content: Insurance adjusters and juries sometimes assume motorcyclists were speeding or riding aggressively — even when no evidence supports this. This bias can reduce settlement offers and damage jury outcomes. Counter it proactively: gather evidence of your sober, licensed, appropriately-geared riding. Your licensing and registration records, any dashcam or GoPro footage from your ride, and witness accounts of your speed and lane position all help build the factual record. Avoid posting about the accident on social media, where any photo or comment can be taken out of context. Your motorcycle safety course certification, helmet, and proper protective gear are all signals of responsible riding behavior — document them.
tips: Collect any dashcam, GoPro, or traffic camera footage as soon as possible
Your safety gear shows responsible riding behavior — document it before discarding
Do not post about the crash on social media — anything you share can be used against you

sections.heading: Seek Medical Evaluation and Ongoing Care
sections.content: Motorcycle injuries frequently require specialist evaluation beyond initial emergency care. Orthopedic surgeons, neurologists, and physical therapists may all become part of your treatment team. Follow every referral and recommendation — gaps in treatment give insurers grounds to argue your injuries resolved or were not serious. Traumatic brain injuries from motorcycle crashes are often underdiagnosed initially; if you experience headaches, cognitive fog, mood changes, or sleep disruption in the days following the crash, report these symptoms to your doctor immediately and request a neurological evaluation. Road rash injuries, while painful, can also conceal deeper soft tissue damage and nerve injury that may require ongoing treatment.
tips: Report all symptoms including headache, dizziness, or memory issues after the crash
Attend every scheduled appointment and all specialist referrals
Keep a daily symptom journal, especially if you have a potential head injury

sections.heading: Know California and Arizona Helmet and Lane Splitting Laws
sections.content: In California, helmet use is mandatory for all motorcycle riders and passengers. Lane splitting — riding between lanes of slow or stopped traffic — is legal in California but must be done safely. In Arizona, helmet use is required for riders under 18; adults over 18 may ride without a helmet if they carry minimum insurance. Lane splitting is not legal in Arizona. If you were involved in lane splitting at the time of the crash in California, this does not automatically establish fault, but it may be a factor considered in the comparative fault analysis. Understanding how these laws apply to your situation affects how your claim is evaluated and negotiated.
tips: California lane splitting is legal but 'safe and prudent' is a factual determination
Arizona prohibits lane splitting entirely — this may affect fault allocation
California helmet law: required for all riders regardless of age

sections.heading: Damages Unique to Motorcycle Accidents
sections.content: Motorcycle accident claims typically involve a broader range of damages than standard car accident claims. In addition to medical bills and lost wages, you may be entitled to compensation for: the cost of replacing or repairing your motorcycle and protective gear; the cost of specialized rehabilitation for road rash, orthopedic injuries, or neurological damage; future medical care if injuries require long-term treatment; lost riding ability if permanent injury prevents you from riding again; and non-economic damages including significant pain and suffering, which are often substantial in motorcycle cases given the severity of typical injuries. Documenting every element of your losses from the beginning — every receipt, every diagnosis, every day of missed work — is essential to capturing the full scope of your damages.
tips: Save all receipts for gear replacement — helmet, jacket, gloves, boots are compensable
Document your riding history and the value of motorcycling to your life if you can no longer ride
Future medical costs must be documented by your treating physician before settling

sections.heading: Dealing With Insurance Companies After a Motorcycle Crash
sections.content: Insurance companies often approach motorcycle claims with heightened skepticism. Some adjusters apply an implicit assumption that the rider was at fault or was riding aggressively, which affects early claim handling. Do not give a recorded statement to the other driver's insurer without understanding your rights — you are generally not required to do so. If you do speak with an adjuster, describe what happened factually and avoid minimizing or speculating about your injuries. Your own insurer may provide uninsured or underinsured motorist coverage if the at-fault driver is not adequately insured, which is unfortunately common. Review your own policy carefully before accepting any settlement from the other party's insurer.
tips: You are generally not required to give a recorded statement to the other driver's insurer
Review your own UM/UIM coverage before accepting any settlement — it may be relevant
Avoid phrases like 'I'm fine' or 'it wasn't that bad' — these minimize your legitimate injuries

sections.heading: When to Consult a Personal Injury Attorney
sections.content: Motorcycle accident cases benefit significantly from legal representation because of the severity of typical injuries, the complexity of comparative fault arguments, and the rider-bias challenge. An attorney experienced in motorcycle cases knows how to anticipate and counter bias arguments, how to gather and preserve the specific evidence that matters (GoPro footage, eyewitness accounts of lane position and speed, traffic camera data), and how to value the full range of damages including future care and non-economic losses. Most personal injury attorneys offer free initial consultations and handle cases on a contingency basis — meaning no upfront cost. Consulting early in the process, before giving any recorded statements or accepting any offers, is worth the time.
tips: Motorcycle cases are more complex than car cases — attorney involvement is often valuable earlier
A free consultation before giving any recorded statement protects your options
Contingency fee arrangements mean no upfront cost to retain a personal injury attorney

sections.heading: Protecting Your Financial Recovery
sections.content: Motorcycle accident victims often face a longer and more complicated financial recovery than car accident victims because of the severity of injuries, extended treatment timelines, and the challenges of rider bias. Protect your financial recovery by documenting every expense from day one: medical bills, gear replacement, motorcycle repairs or replacement, transportation to appointments, and any modifications to your home or vehicle required by your injuries. Lost wages — including overtime, bonuses, and self-employment income — require documentation through pay stubs, tax returns, and employer letters. If your injuries are permanent or long-term, future earning capacity and future medical costs become major components of your damages. These require expert documentation — vocational rehabilitation specialists and life care planners may be part of your attorney's case-building team. Start the financial record on day one rather than trying to reconstruct it months later.
tips: Document every accident-related expense from the day of the crash
Gear replacement — helmet, jacket, gloves, boots — is compensable damage
Future medical costs and lost earning capacity require expert documentation in serious cases
